title: RMSE: Root Mean Squared Error
concept: Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) takes the square root of MSE, converting a squared-unit error score back into the model's original, interpretable units — like dollars instead of squared dollars.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Why can't you use "accuracy" to grade a regression model?

options.text: Because regression predicts continuous numbers, and exact matches are rare even for great predictions
Because regression models never make mistakes
Because accuracy is only used for images

exercises.explain: Right — regression needs distance-based metrics instead. Today: the most common one, RMSE.

cards.title: "Off by 2.5 billion squared dollars"
cards.body: A report says your house-price model has an error of 2,500,000,000 squared dollars. That number is technically correct — and completely useless to a homeowner.

cards.title: What is RMSE?
cards.body: Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) is the square root of MSE. Squaring the errors puts them in squared units; taking the square root brings them back to the model's original units.

cards.title: Area back to a side length
cards.body: MSE is like reporting the area of a square room (in square feet) when someone asked how long one wall is (in feet). RMSE takes the square root — the same move as turning square feet back into feet.

cards.title: From squared dollars to dollars
cards.body: If a house-price model has an MSE of 2,500,000,000 dollars², its RMSE is √2,500,000,000 ≈ $50,000. That means predictions are typically off by about $50,000 — a number you can actually explain to a client.

cards.title: Not a new formula
cards.body: RMSE isn't a different calculation from MSE — it's the exact same squared errors, just with one extra step (√) at the end. The goal shifts too: MSE guides training, while RMSE reports results after training is done.

left.text:
Squared units (dollars²)
Used during training to guide optimization

right.text:
Original units (dollars)
Used after training to report model quality

exercises.prompt: If a model's MSE is 64 (in squared units), what is its RMSE?
exercises.explain: RMSE is the square root of MSE. The square root of 64 is 8.

exercises.prompt: True or false: RMSE is reported in the same units as the original prediction (like dollars or degrees), while MSE is reported in squared units.
exercises.explain: True! Taking the square root of MSE converts squared units back into the model's original, interpretable units.

exercises.sentence: RMSE is calculated by taking the ___ of MSE.
exercises.explain: RMSE = √MSE. Taking the square root converts the squared-unit error back into the original units.

exercises.prompt: A model has an MSE of 100 dollars². Which of these is its RMSE, in dollars?
exercises.explain: √100 = 10, so the RMSE is $10 — meaning predictions are typically off by about $10.

exercises.prompt: Two stakeholders review the same house-price model. One sees "MSE = 6,400,000,000" and is confused. The other sees "RMSE = $80,000" and understands immediately. Why does converting to RMSE help here?

options.text: RMSE undoes the squaring, expressing the typical error in dollars — the same unit as the price being predicted, which stakeholders can interpret directly
RMSE is a completely different metric that ignores large errors
MSE and RMSE always give the same number, so it doesn't matter which one you report
RMSE rounds the price predictions to the nearest thousand dollars

exercises.explain: Exactly! RMSE takes the square root of MSE, turning squared dollars back into plain dollars — a number anyone can understand. Next: a metric that scores your model from 0 to 1.
